coach_note:
Mid-April update. Chris raced a 10K on 12 April in 39:37 — a massive result that neither of us expected this early in the block. He went out hard behind a group on a downhill start into the wind, settled at 4:00/km, wobbled at 3.5km thinking he couldn't sustain it, got pulled along by a clubmate, found control at 6km, then rallied through a tough headwind finish. Average HR 150 (91.5% max), max 157 (95.7% max) — a properly executed race effort that shows his aerobic engine is significantly further along than the 20:00 5K estimate from two weeks ago.

The 39:37 10K equates to roughly 19:00-19:15 5K fitness right now, possibly faster on a good day. His own target of sub-19:00 at Harwich on 28 April is realistic. He said he wasn't completely drained after — there may have been a few more seconds in the tank without the headwind.

The training data tells the same story. He's executing sessions well: sub-threshold reps at 141-142bpm avg HR (right in the 86-91% zone), easy runs genuinely easy at 97-111bpm, and his Garmin self-evaluations show he's rating sessions appropriately (RPE 1 for easy, 3-4 for quality, 8 for the race). CTL has jumped from 29 to 36.6 in two weeks — a sharp but sustainable ramp given the low starting point and the fact that he's responding well.

Wellness markers are all green. HRV has made a significant jump into the 60s-70s range (was in the 50s two weeks ago). Resting HR has dropped to 33-35bpm. VO2max estimate up to 61. Weight back to 70.8kg (the 76.2kg artefact has cleared). Sleep is still variable (scores 72-94) but adequate. He's feeling great and his confidence is building — that race was exactly the boost he needed after four months of struggle.

Plan adjustment: the Bromley 10K that was scheduled as a fun run on 12 April became this 10K race instead (Tendring, not Bromley — may have been a late switch). The week's structure held well around it. No changes needed to the rest of the plan — the freshen week (wk3) into Harwich 5K (wk4 Tue 28 Apr) is perfectly timed. If anything, this result means we can be more ambitious with the 5K target.
wellness_notes: Wellness data as of 14 April 2026 — all markers trending positively. Resting HR: 33-36bpm over the last two weeks, with a clear downward trend from 37-38 at the start of April to 33-35 now. This is a strong recovery and adaptation signal. HRV: significant improvement — now consistently in the 60-77 range compared to mid-50s two weeks ago. The 77 reading on 11 April is Chris's highest in the data we have. Only dips to 51-56 on days after harder sessions, which is a normal acute response. CTL: 36.6 and climbing fast (was 29 on 2 April). Ramp rate is high at +6.3 but appropriate given the low starting point and the training load being mostly aerobic. ATL at 57 reflects the recent race loading. Weight: 70.8kg confirmed on 7 April — the 76.2kg artefact from 27 March has been resolved. VO2max: Garmin estimate now 61, up from 58-59 pre-plan. Sleep: scores 72-94, quality variable (mix of 1-3 ratings). Average duration ~6.5-7 hours. Adequate but could be better — consistent bedtime would help.
upcoming_training_notes:
10-week Norwegian Singles plan is live on the Intervals.icu calendar (6 Apr – 14 Jun 2026). Currently in week 2 of the block.

Immediate focus: Wk3 (20-26 Apr) is the freshen week before Harwich 5K Race 1 on Tue 28 April. Volume drops to 45km with the benchmark 3x3km on Thursday and reduced easy running. The goal is to arrive at the race fresh and confident — Chris is already feeling both.

Race schedule: 28 Apr Harwich 5K R1 (target: sub-19:00), 10 May Colchester 10K (marker race, no taper), 19 May Harwich 5K R2, 9 Jun Harwich 5K R3.

Benchmark: 3x3km sub-threshold appears in weeks 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 10. First benchmark is Thu 23 April — this will give us a baseline to track pace at 86-91% max HR.

HR zone calibration: The 10K race data gives us a much better picture. Chris averaged 150bpm over 39:37 at race effort — this is right at his Garmin-estimated LTHR of 149. His max in the race was 157 vs Garmin's 164 max HR estimate. This suggests either the max HR estimate is slightly high or he had headroom. Worth watching the Harwich 5K data for a higher max HR reading to calibrate.

Strength: Still needs attention. No strength sessions logged in the last two weeks. Minimum 1-2 per week.
march_feedback_summary: Holiday was the turning point. Before it, every run was a struggle. Since returning from Tenerife, every run has felt like a step forward. Strength training neglected — acknowledged and wants to address. Sleep inconsistent. Energy improving. Wants to avoid overdoing it while not underdoing it.

garmin_self_evaluations.summary: Chris is using Garmin RPE and feel ratings consistently. Pattern shows good self-awareness: easy runs rated RPE 1 / feel 2-3, sub-threshold sessions RPE 3-4 / feel 1-2, race RPE 8 / feel 2. The feel ratings suggest he's finding the quality sessions satisfying (feel 1 = good) and the easy runs comfortable. The race was hard but not devastating (feel 2 = ok, not great not awful).

predictions.rationale: 39:37 10K on 12 April equates to ~19:00-19:15 5K. Two more weeks of training plus a proper freshen week should unlock sub-19. Chris woke up feeling confident on race day — motivation and belief are high. Flat course at Harwich is an advantage over the Tendring 10K course.

If Harwich goes well and training continues, 4 more weeks of build should bring the 10K down by 30-60 seconds from the 39:37. Colchester is a fast course. No taper — this is off normal training volume.

7 weeks into the block with CTL likely approaching 40+. If benchmark sessions show continued pace improvement at the same HR, sub-18:30 is a realistic stretch target. This is where the Norwegian Singles work should really start paying off.
